text: package, user can estimate steady-state BVAR model Villani (2009). steady-state BVAR simply BVAR rewritten mean-adjusted form. benefit mean-adjusted parametrization allows user specify prior beliefs unconditional mean, steady state VAR system. model proven useful forecasting macroeconomic variables, routinely used many central banks finanicial institutions (Gustafsson Villani, 2025). estimation, user can produce forecasts (unconditional conditional) impulse response functions (orthogonalized generalized). goal SteadyStateBVAR use modern Bayesian tools (Stan) : ) estimate model specified original paper, ii) extend model different ways. Previously, extensions model seemed limited Mattias Villani time derive. See example Clark (2011), extends steady-state BVAR model include Random Walk stochastic volatility: “methodological sense, paper extends estimator Villani (2009) include stochastic volatility.” Later , writes: “(Special thanks due Mattias Villani providing formulas posterior means variances \(\Pi\) \(\Psi\), generalize constant-variance formulas Villani 2009.)” Another example Dieppe, Legrand, van Roye (2016): “Villani (2009) provides derivation case normal-diffuse prior distribution, incoming analysis restricted case.” Times different now, help Stan, essentially limited imagination. time writing, package provides three versions steady-state BVAR model: ) homoscedastic model, .e. original model Villani (2009), ii) Random Walk stochastic volatility model, .e. one Clark (2011), iii) AR(1) stochastic volatility model.

title: Bayesian Vector Autoregressions with Steady-State Priors
text: steady-state BVAR(\(p\)) model (Villani, 2009) \[y_t = \Psi d_t + \Pi_1(y_{t-1}-\Psi d_{t-1})+\dots+\Pi_p(y_{t-p}-\Psi d_{t-p})+u_t\] \(y_t\) \(k\)-dimensional vector endogenous variables (time series) time \(t\), \(d_t\) \(q\)-dimensional vector deterministic (exogenous) variables time \(t\), (reduced-form) innovations \(u_t \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} \mathrm{N_k}(0,\Sigma_u)\). \(\Pi_\ell\) \(\ell=1,\dots,p\) \((k \times k)\) autoregressive parameter matrix, \(\Psi\) \((k \times q)\) steady-state parameter matrix. Now \[\mathrm{E}(y_t)=\mu_t=\Psi d_t\] unconditional mean, steady state, process. Since long-horizon forecasts stationary VARs converge unconditional mean (steady state), naturally important forecasting perspective obtain precise inference \(\Psi\). Note current version package allows \(d_t\) contain either constant, constant dummy variable, constant time trend. may stack (transposed) \(\Pi_i\) matrices \((kp \times k)\) matrix \(\beta\) \[\beta= \begin{bmatrix} \Pi'_1 \\ \vdots  \\ \Pi'_p \end{bmatrix}\] can rewrite model nonlinear regression (Karlsson, 2013) \[y_t' =d_t'\Psi' + \left[w_t'-q_t'(I_p \otimes \Psi') \right]\beta +u_t'\] \(w_t'=(y_{t-1}',\dots,y_{t-p}')\) \(kp\)-dimensional vector lagged endogenous variables \(q_t'=(d_{t-1}',\dots,d_{t-p}')\) \(qp\)-dimensional vector lagged deterministic (exogenous) variables, \(I_p\) \((p \times p)\) identity matrix \(\otimes\) denotes Kronecker product. likelihood written Stan code. goal estimate \(\beta, \Psi\), \(\Sigma_u\), priors needed. Following Villani (2009), prior independence \(\beta, \Psi\) \(\Sigma_u\) assumed. \(\beta\), Minnesota prior used \[\mathrm{vec}(\beta) \sim \mathrm{N}_{kpk} \left[\theta_\beta,\Omega_\beta\right]\] prior means (elements \(\theta_\beta\)) set \[\begin{aligned} \mathrm{E}\left(\Pi_{\ell}^{(,j)}\right)&= \begin{cases} \kappa & \text{} \ell = 1 \ \mathrm{} \ = j \\ 0 & \mathrm{otherwise} \end{cases}\\ \kappa&= \begin{cases} \kappa^{level} & \text{} \mathrm{variable} \ \ \mathrm{level} \\ \kappa^{\Delta} & \text{}\mathrm{variable} \ \ \mathrm{difference} \end{cases}\\ \end{aligned}\] , autoregressive coefficient \(\Pi_{\ell}^{(,j)}\) element \(\left(,j\right)\) \(\Pi_{\ell}\) \(\ell=1,\dots,p\). , Minnesota prior sets prior means elements \(\beta\) \(0\), except elements relate first lags variables, set \(\kappa\). variable \(\) level (e.g. nominal interest rate), \(\kappa=\kappa^{level}\), typical choices \(\kappa^{level}\) \(1\) \(0.9\). Evaluating equations prior means, equation \(\) becomes random walk \(\kappa^{level}=1\) persistent stationary AR(1) process \(\kappa^{level}=0.9\). Since steady state exists process stationary, \(0.9\) recommended steady-state BVAR. variable \(\) difference (e.g. output growth), \(\kappa=\kappa^{\Delta}\), common choice \(\kappa^{\Delta}\) \(0\), .e. equation \(\) becomes (evaluating prior means) random walk expressed first differences. differenced variable still shows degree persistence (can examined ACF plot), suitable value \(\kappa^{\Delta}\) can (example) \(0.6\) instead \(0\). Moving prior variances, \(\Omega_\beta\) diagonal matrix containing prior variances elements \(\beta\). specified \[\mathrm{Var}\left(\Pi_{\ell}^{(,j)}\right)= \begin{cases} \left(\frac{\lambda_1}{\ell^{\lambda_3}}\right)^2 & \text{} = j \\ \left(\frac{\lambda_1 \lambda_2\sigma_i}{\ell^{\lambda_3}\sigma_j}\right)^2& \text{} \neq j \end{cases}\] \(\lambda_1\), \(\lambda_2\), \(\lambda_3\) scalar hyperparameters known overall tightness, cross-equation tightness lag decay rate. Furthermore, \(\sigma_i^2\) \((,)\):th element \(\Sigma_u\), know therefore replace estimate. package, replaced least squares residual variance univariate autoregression variable \(\) \(p\) lags (including constant dummy/trend variable applicable). Moving \(\Psi\), contains steady-state parameters, prior \[\mathrm{vec}(\Psi) \sim \mathrm{N}_{kq}\left[\theta_\Psi,\Omega_\Psi\right]\] core steady-state BVAR model. \(\theta_\Psi\), specify prior beliefs location steady state, \(\Omega_\Psi\), assume diagonal matrix, specify degree certainty prior beliefs. Finally, prior \(\Sigma_u\) usual non-informative Jeffreys prior \[p(\Sigma_u) \propto\left|\Sigma_u \right|^{-(k+1)/2}\] Alternatively, proper inverse-Wishart prior can used (Karlsson, 2013) \[\Sigma_u \sim \mathrm{IW}(V,m)\] \(V\) scale matrix \(m\geq k+2\) number degrees freedom. mentioned, package also allows stochastic volatility (Random Walk AR(1) specifications), covariance matrix varies time, .e. \(\Sigma_{u,t}\) (see ?bvar details).

start(yt), frequency = frequency(yt))  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  #Use a dummy to model Sweden’s change in monetary policy in the 1990s #(move to inflation targeting and flexible exchange rate) bp <- which(time(yt) == 1992.75) #breakpoint dummy_variable <- c(rep(1,bp), rep(0,nrow(yt)-bp))  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj,                   p=4,                   deterministic = "constant_and_dummy", Table I in Villani (2009) #These are the "steady-state priors" #psi_1 = Psi col 1 #psi_2 = Psi col 2  theta_Psi <-    c(   ppi( 2.00,  3.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_1: delta y_f annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$mean,  #psi_1: q   ppi(-1.00,  1.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_2: delta y_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_2: pi_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$mean,  #psi_2: i_f   ppi(-1.00,  1.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_2: delta y   ppi( 4.30,  5.70,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_2: annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_1: delta y_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_1: pi_f   ppi( 4.50,  5.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var,   #psi_1: i_f   ppi( #psi_2: pi_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var,   #psi_2: i_f   ppi(-1.00,  1.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_2: delta y   ppi( 4.30,  5.70,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_2: pi   ppi( 3.00,  5.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var,   #psi_2: i   ppi(-0.50,  0.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var    #psi_2: q   )   )  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    lambda_1,                    lambda_2,                    lambda_3,                    fol_pm,                    theta_Psi,                    Omega_Psi,                    Jeffreys=TRUE) #FALSE for uninformative inverse-Wishart  p <- bvar_obj$setup$p k <- bvar_obj$setup$k kf <- 3 #first three variables in yt are foreign  restriction_matrix <- matrix(1, k*p, k)  for(i in 1:p){   rows <- ((i-1)*k + kf + 1) : (i*k)   cols <- 1:kf   restriction_matrix[rows, cols] <- 0 } print(restriction_matrix) #block exogeneity for foreign variables bvar_obj <- restrict_beta(bvar_obj, restriction_matrix)  H <- 12 #forecast horizon (d_pred <- cbind(rep(1, 12), 0)) #future d_t values   #fit the model bvar_obj <- fit(bvar_obj,                 H = H,                 d_pred = d_pred,                 iter = 10000,                 warmup = 2500,                 chains = 2,                 cores = 2)  #posterior summaries summary(bvar_obj , stat = "mean")  #you can look at the stanfit object directly stan_fit <- bvar_obj$fit$stan print(stan_fit)  #unconditional forecasts #see last forecasts in Figures 1-3 in Villani (2009) fcst <- forecast(bvar_obj,                  pi = 0.95, #pi = prediction interval                  fcst_type = "mean",                  growth_rate_idx = c(4,5), #convert QoQ forecasts to YoY                  plot_idx = c(4,5,6))  #conditional forecasts #Toy scenario: inflation gets really high #What will happen to domestic interest rate? conditions <- data.frame(               var        = rep(5,12),               horizon    = rep(1:12),               value      = c(1.0,1.5,2.0,1.8,                              1.5,1.2,1.0,1.0,                              rep(0.5,4)) #QoQ scale for inflation here               )                cond_fcst <- conditional_forecast(bvar_obj,                     conditions,                     pi=0.68,                     fcst_type = "mean",                     plot_idx = c(5,6),                     growth_rate_idx = c(5)) #convert QoQ forecasts to YoY  #impulse response analysis irf <- IRF(bvar_obj,            H=20,            response=5,#inflation            shock=6, #interest rate            type="median",            method="OIRF",            ci=0.68,            growth_rate_idx=5) #YoY inflation instead of QoQ

title: Create a steady-state BVAR model object — bvar
text:
model takes form $$y_t = \Psi d_t + \Pi_1(y_{t-1}-\Psi d_{t-1})+\dots+\Pi_p(y_{t-p}-\Psi d_{t-p})+u_t$$ \(y_t\) \(k\)-dimensional vector endogenous variables time \(t\), \(d_t\) \(q\)-dimensional vector deterministic (exogenous) variables time \(t\). \(\Pi_\ell\) \(\ell=1,\dots,p\) \((k 	imes k)\) matrix autoregressive parameters, \(\Psi\) \((k 	imes q)\) matrix steady-state parameters. Note \(\mathrm{E}(y_t)=\mu_t=\Psi d_t\) unconditional mean, steady state process. One can stack (transposed) \(\Pi_i\) matrices \((kp 	imes k)\) matrix \( eta\) $$ eta= egin{bmatrix}\Pi'_1 \ \vdots  \\Pi'_p\end{bmatrix}$$ model can rewritten nonlinear regression (Karlsson, 2013) $$y_t' =d_t'\Psi' + \left[w_t'-q_t'(I_p \otimes \Psi') ight] eta +u_t'$$ \(w_t'=(y_{t-1}',\dots,y_{t-p}')\) \(kp\)-dimensional vector lagged endogenous variables \(q_t'=(d_{t-1}',\dots,d_{t-p}')\) \(qp\)-dimensional vector lagged deterministic (exogenous) variables, \(I_p\) \((p 	imes p)\) identity matrix \(\otimes\) denotes Kronecker product. likelihood written Stan code. goal estimate \( eta, \Psi\), \(\Sigma_u\). innovations model, case homoscedastic steady-state BVAR, \(u_t \overset{	ext{iid}}{\sim} \mathrm{N_k}(0,\Sigma_u)\). However, models stochastic volatility, instead time-varying covariance matrix \(u_t \sim \mathrm{N_k}(0,\Sigma_{u,t})\). innovations take form $$ egin{aligned} u_t &= ^{-1} \Lambda^{0.5}_t \epsilon_t \ \epsilon_t &\overset{	ext{iid}}{\sim} \mathrm{N}(0, \mathrm{}_k)\end{aligned}$$ \(\) lower triangular matrix ones diagonal describes contemporaneous interaction endogenous variables, $$\Lambda_t = \mathrm{diag}(\lambda_{1,t},\dots,\lambda_{k,t})$$ contains time-varying volatilities. AR1 stochastic volatility specification, log volatilities follow AR(1) processes $$\ln \lambda_{,t} = \gamma_{0,} + \gamma_{1,} \ln \lambda_{,t-1} + 
u_{,t}, \ =1,\dots,k$$ log volatility AR(1) processes restricted stationary region, .e. \(|\gamma_{1,}|<1 \  orall \). RW stochastic volatility specification, log volatilities follow Random Walk processes $$\gamma_{0,}=0, \ \gamma_{1,}=1 \  orall $$ innovations log volatilities follow AR1 case $$
u_{t} = (
u_{1,t},\dots,
u_{k,t})'\overset{	ext{iid}}{\sim} \mathrm{N}(0, \Phi)$$ \(\Phi\) diagonal innovations log volatilities allowed correlated across variables. RW case, \(\Phi\) diagonal variances \(\phi_i\) \(=1,\dots,k\). Note stochastic volatility specifications, time-varying covariance matrix $$\Sigma_{u,t} = ^{-1} \Lambda_t (^{-1})'$$ details homoscedastic steady-state BVAR model, see Villani (2009). details Random Walk stochastic volatility steady-state BVAR model, see Clark (2011). See Carriero, Clark, Marcellino (2024) -mentioned AR(1) stochastic volatility specification applied conventional BVAR.

title: Forecast from a fitted steady-state BVAR model — forecast
text: Computes plots forecasts fitted bvar object. Draws joint predictive distribution used construct point forecasts prediction intervals. Optionally converts monthly quarterly growth rate forecasts annual growth rates selected variables.

title: Forecast from a fitted steady-state BVAR model — forecast
text: x steady-state bvar object passed fit. pi Numeric. prediction interval width. Default 0.95, .e. 95% prediction interval. fcst_type Character. Whether use "mean" "median" point forecast. Default "mean". growth_rate_idx Integer vector. Indices variables convert forecasts annual growth rates \(\ln x_{t} - \ln x_{t-f}\), \(f\) frequency data (4 quarterly, 12 monthly). suitable variables specified \(\ln x_{t} - \ln x_{t-1}\), .e. diff(log(x)) 100*diff(log(x)). Computed summing \(f\) log first differences. Default NULL. plot_idx Integer vector. Indices variables plot. NULL (default), variables plotted. Forecasts always computed returned variables, regardless plot_idx. show_all Logical. FALSE (default), last two years history shown alongside forecast. TRUE, full history shown.

what: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
text: Calculates mean variance normal prior probability interval. Given lower upper bound prior probability interval function recovers implied normal prior parameters. Useful specifying informative priors steady-state parameters (elements \(\Psi\)).

title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
code: ppi(l, u, interval = 0.95, annualized_growthrate = FALSE, freq = 4)

title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
text: l Numeric. lower bound prior probability interval. u Numeric. upper bound prior probability interval. interval Numeric. prior probability mass within interval. Default 0.95, .e. 95%. annualized_growthrate Logical. TRUE, treats l u bounds annualized steady-state growth rate calculates implied mean variance corresponding quarterly/monthly scale. Useful working variable diff(log(x)) (may scaled 100). Default FALSE. freq Integer. data frequency (e.g. 4 quarterly). used annualized_growthrate = TRUE. Default 4.

title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
text: list two elements: mean var, giving mean variance implied normal distribution.

title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
text: Consider CPI variable CPI <- data$CPI, observed quarterly frequency. model, quarter--quarter inflation used x <- 100*diff(log(CPI)). Suppose prior belief annualized steady-state inflation lies 1.7 2.3 95% probability (mean 2). quarterly scale used x, corresponds 95% interval 0.425 0.575 (mean 0.5). Since typically natural elicit prior annualized scale, annualized_growthrate argument performs conversion. See vignette("Homoscedastic-steady-state-BVAR") usage practice.

title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi

what: Specify priors for the steady-state BVAR model — priors
title: Specify priors for the steady-state BVAR model — priors
text: function prepares priors. Minnesota prior used autoregressive parameters, determined overall tightness, cross-equation tightness, lag decay rate. steady-state parameters, normal prior used. covariance matrix innovations, user can choose Jeffreys prior uninformative inverse-Wishart prior. Optionally enables stochastic volatility covariance matrix varies time.

title: Specify priors for the steady-state BVAR model — priors
code: priors(   x,   lambda_1 = 0.2,   lambda_2 = 0.5,   lambda_3 = 1,   first_own_lag_prior_mean = NULL,   theta_Psi = NULL,   Omega_Psi = NULL,   Jeffreys = TRUE,   SV = FALSE,   SV_type = NULL,   SV_priors = NULL )

title: Specify priors for the steady-state BVAR model — priors
text: x steady-state bvar object passed setup. lambda_1 Numeric. Overall tightness Minnesota prior. Default 0.2. lambda_2 Numeric. Cross-equation tightness Minnesota prior. Default 0.5. lambda_3 Numeric. Lag decay rate Minnesota prior. Default 1. first_own_lag_prior_mean Numeric vector length k. Prior means first lags variables. NULL (default), zero vector used. theta_Psi Numeric vector. Prior mean vector \(\text{vec}(\Psi)\), .e. steady-state parameters. NULL (default), OLS estimates used. Omega_Psi Numeric matrix. Prior covariance matrix \(\text{vec}(\Psi)\), .e. steady-state parameters. NULL (default), diagonal matrix variances 1000 used. Jeffreys Logical. TRUE (default), uses Jeffreys prior innovation covariance matrix. FALSE, uses uninformative inverse-Wishart prior. considered SV=FALSE. SV Logical. TRUE, enables stochastic volatility specification. Default FALSE. SV_type Character. Type stochastic volatility model. Must either "RW" "AR1". Required SV = TRUE. SV_priors List. User-supplied stochastic volatility priors. Required SV = TRUE. list must contain following named elements depending SV_type: "RW": theta_A, Omega_A, mu_log_lambda_0, sigma2_log_lambda_0, alpha_phi, beta_phi. "AR1": theta_A, Omega_A, theta_gamma_0, Omega_gamma_0, theta_gamma_1, Omega_gamma_1, theta_log_lambda_0, Omega_log_lambda_0, V_Phi, m_Phi.

title: Specify priors for the steady-state BVAR model — priors
text: steady-state bvar object appended priors list containing: theta_beta Prior mean vector \(\text{vec}(\beta)\) constructed Minnesota prior Omega_beta Prior covariance matrix \(\text{vec}(\beta)\) constructed Minnesota prior theta_Psi Prior mean vector \(\text{vec}(\Psi)\), .e. steady-state parameters Omega_Psi Prior covariance matrix \(\text{vec}(\Psi)\), .e. steady-state parameters Jeffreys Indicator Jeffreys prior usage Sigma_AR Residual variance estimates univariate AR fits, used Minnesota prior m Inverse-Wishart prior degrees freedom (Jeffreys = FALSE) V Inverse-Wishart prior scale matrix (Jeffreys = FALSE) SV Logical indicator stochastic volatility specification SV_type Stochastic volatility specification type SV_priors User-supplied SV prior list (SV = TRUE)

title: Specify priors for the steady-state BVAR model — priors
text: goal estimate \(\beta, \Psi\), \(\Sigma_u\), priors needed. Following Villani (2009), prior independence \(\beta, \Psi\) \(\Sigma_u\) assumed. \(\beta\), .e. autoregressive parameter matrix, Minnesota prior used $$\mathrm{vec}(\beta) \sim \mathrm{N}_{kpk} \left[\theta_\beta,\Omega_\beta\right]$$ prior means (elements \(\theta_\beta\)) set $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{E}\left(\Pi_{\ell}^{(,j)}\right)&= \begin{cases}\kappa & \text{} \ell = 1 \ \text{} \ = j \\0 & \text{otherwise}\end{cases}\\ \kappa&=\begin{cases}\kappa^{level} & \text{} \text{variable} \ \ \text{level} \\ \kappa^{\Delta} & \text{} \text{variable} \ \ \text{difference} \end{cases}\\ \end{aligned} $$ , autoregressive coefficient \(\Pi_{\ell}^{(,j)}\) element \(\left(,j\right)\) \(\Pi_{\ell}\) \(\ell=1,\dots,p\). , Minnesota prior sets prior means elements \(\beta\) \(0\), except elements relate first lags variables, set \(\kappa\). variable \(\) level (e.g. nominal interest rate), \(\kappa=\kappa^{level}\), typical choices \(\kappa^{level}\) \(1\) \(0.9\). Evaluating equations prior means, equation \(\) becomes random walk \(\kappa^{level}=1\) persistent stationary AR(1) process \(\kappa^{level}=0.9\). Since steady state exists process stationary, \(0.9\) recommended steady-state BVAR. variable \(\) difference (e.g. output growth), \(\kappa=\kappa^{\Delta}\), common choice \(\kappa^{\Delta}\) \(0\), .e. equation \(\) becomes (evaluating prior means) random walk expressed first differences. differenced variable still shows degree persistence (can examined ACF plot), suitable value \(\kappa^{\Delta}\) can (example) \(0.6\) instead \(0\). Moving prior variances, \(\Omega_\beta\) diagonal matrix containing prior variances elements \(\beta\). specified $$\mathrm{Var}\left(\Pi_{\ell}^{(,j)}\right)= \begin{cases}\left(\frac{\lambda_1}{\ell^{\lambda_3}}\right)^2 & \text{} = j \\ \left(\frac{\lambda_1 \lambda_2\sigma_i}{\ell^{\lambda_3}\sigma_j}\right)^2& \text{} \neq j \end{cases}$$ \(\lambda_1\), \(\lambda_2\), \(\lambda_3\) scalar hyperparameters known overall tightness, cross-equation tightness lag decay rate. Furthermore, \(\sigma_i^2\) \((,)\):th element \(\Sigma_u\), unknown therefore replaced estimate. package, replaced least squares residual variance univariate autoregression variable \(\) \(p\) lags (including constant dummy/trend variable applicable). Moving \(\Psi\), steady-state parameter matrix, prior $$\mathrm{vec}(\Psi) \sim \mathrm{N}_{kq}\left[\theta_\Psi,\Omega_\Psi\right]$$ core steady-state BVAR model. \(\theta_\Psi\), one specifies prior beliefs location steady state, \(\Omega_\Psi\), assumed diagonal matrix, one specifies degree certainty prior beliefs. prior \(\Sigma_u\) either usual non-informative Jeffreys prior $$p(\Sigma_u) \propto\left|\Sigma_u \right|^{-(k+1)/2}$$ proper uninformative inverse-Wishart prior $$\Sigma_u \sim \mathrm{IW}(V,m)$$ \(V\) scale matrix \(m\geq k+2\) number degrees freedom. uninformative prior specified setting \(V=(m-k-1)\hat{\Sigma}_u\) \(\hat{\Sigma}_u\) least squares estimate VAR(\(p\)) (including constant dummy/trend variable applicable), \(m=k+2\). stochastic volatility specifications, innovation covariance matrix now time-varying \(\Sigma_{u,t}\). Therefore, stochastic volatility priors needed, see bvar details. Random Walk (RW) stochastic volatility specification, following priors used $$\begin{aligned}&\sim \mathrm{N}(\theta_A, \Omega_A) \\ \ln \lambda_{,0} &\sim \mathrm{N}(\mu_{\ln \lambda_{,0}}, \sigma^2_{\ln \lambda_{,0}}) \\ \phi_i &\sim \mathrm{IG}(\alpha_{\phi_i},\beta_{\phi_i})\end{aligned}$$ \(\) \(k(k-1)/2\) vector collects free parameters \(\) row-major order, \(\ln \lambda_{,0}\) time \(t=0\) values (initial conditions) \(\ln \lambda_{,t}\). Furthermore, \(\phi_i\) log volatility innovation variances. AR(1) (AR1) stochastic volatility specification, following priors used $$\begin{aligned}&\sim \mathrm{N}(\theta_A, \Omega_A) \\ \gamma_{0} &\sim \mathrm{N}(\theta_{\gamma_0}, \Omega_{\gamma_0}) \\ \gamma_{1} &\sim \mathrm{N}(\theta_{\gamma_1}, \Omega_{\gamma_1}) \\ \ln \lambda_{0} &\sim \mathrm{N}(\theta_{\ln \lambda_{0}}, \Omega_{\ln \lambda_{0}}) \\ \Phi &\sim \mathrm{IW}(V_{\Phi},m_{\Phi})\end{aligned}$$ \(\) \(k(k-1)/2\) vector collects free parameters \(\) row-major order, \(\ln \lambda_0\) time \(t=0\) values (initial conditions) \(\ln \lambda_{t}\). Furthermore, \(\gamma_{0}\) log volatility intercepts, \(\gamma_{1}\) log volatility slopes, \(\Phi\) log volatility innovation covariance matrix.

code: #homoscedastic with Jeffreys prior yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p=1)  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    lambda_1 = 0.2,                    lambda_2 = 0.5,                    lambda_3 = 1,                    first_own_lag_prior_mean = rep(1,2),                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2),                    Jeffreys = TRUE,                    SV = FALSE,                    SV_type = NULL,                    SV_priors = NULL)                     #RW stochastic volatility yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p=1)  k <- bvar_obj$setup$k n_free_params_A <- bvar_obj$setup$n_free_params_A  SV_priors_RW <- list( theta_A              =  rep(0, n_free_params_A), Omega_A              =  diag(1000, n_free_params_A), mu_log_lambda_0      =  rep(0, k), sigma2_log_lambda_0  =  rep(1000, k), alpha_phi            =  rep(5, k), beta_phi             = (rep(5, k) - 1) * rep(0.1, k) )  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    lambda_1 = 0.2,                    lambda_2 = 0.5,                    lambda_3 = 1,                    first_own_lag_prior_mean = rep(1,2),                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2),                    SV = TRUE,                    SV_type = "RW",                    SV_priors = SV_priors_RW)                     #AR1 stochastic volatility yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p=1)  k <- bvar_obj$setup$k n_free_params_A <- bvar_obj$setup$n_free_params_A  SV_priors_AR1 <- list( theta_A               =  rep(0, n_free_params_A), Omega_A               =  diag(1000, n_free_params_A), theta_gamma_0         =  rep(0.1, k), Omega_gamma_0         =  diag(1000, k), theta_gamma_1         =  rep(0.9, k), Omega_gamma_1         =  diag(10, k), theta_log_lambda_0    =  rep(0.1, k)/(1-rep(0.9, k)), Omega_log_lambda_0    =  diag(1000, k), V_Phi                 = (10 - k - 1) * diag(k), m_Phi                 =  10 )  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    lambda_1 = 0.2,                    lambda_2 = 0.5,                    lambda_3 = 1,                    first_own_lag_prior_mean = rep(1,2),                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2),                    SV = TRUE,                    SV_type = "AR1",                    SV_priors = SV_priors_AR1)

what: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
text: Applies zero restrictions VAR coefficient matrix \(\beta\) setting corresponding prior variances Omega_beta value near zero (prior means zero default Minnesota prior).

title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
code: restrict_beta(x, restriction_matrix)

title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
text: x steady-state bvar object passed priors. restriction_matrix numeric matrix dimension k*p x k entries 0 indicate coefficients restricted zero entries 1 indicate unrestricted coefficients.

title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
text: steady-state bvar object restriction matrix stored setup prior covariance matrix Omega_beta updated accordingly.

title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
text: steady-state BVAR model takes form $$y_t = \Psi d_t + \Pi_1(y_{t-1}-\Psi d_{t-1})+\dots+\Pi_p(y_{t-p}-\Psi d_{t-p})+u_t$$ \(y_t\) \(k\)-dimensional vector endogenous variables time \(t\), \(d_t\) \(q\)-dimensional vector deterministic (exogenous) variables time \(t\). \(\Pi_\ell\) \(\ell=1,\dots,p\) \((k \times k)\) matrix autoregressive parameters, \(\Psi\) \((k \times q)\) matrix steady-state parameters. One can stack (transposed) \(\Pi_i\) matrices \((kp \times k)\) matrix \(\beta\) $$\beta=\begin{bmatrix}\Pi'_1 \\ \vdots  \\\Pi'_p\end{bmatrix}$$ function puts zero restrictions elements \(\beta\).

title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
code: yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p=2)  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2))                     p <- bvar_obj$setup$p k <- bvar_obj$setup$k  restriction_matrix <- matrix(1,k*p,k, dimnames=list(NULL,c("y1","y2")))  #restrict beta so y2 does not granger-cause y1  restriction_matrix[2, 1] <- 0 restriction_matrix[4, 1] <- 0  print(restriction_matrix) #>      y1 y2 #> [1,]  1  1 #> [2,]  0  1 #> [3,]  1  1 #> [4,]  0  1  bvar_obj <- restrict_beta(bvar_obj, restriction_matrix)

text: Returns input object invisibly.

title: Summarise a fitted steady-state BVAR model — summary.bvar
text: function summarises following estimated parameters: beta: \(kp \times k\) VAR coefficient matrix Psi: \(k \times q\) steady-state parameter matrix Sigma_u: innovation covariance matrix (\(k \times k\) homoscedastic, \(T \times k \times k\) stochastic volatility) Random Walk stochastic volatility: : \(k \times k\) lower triangular matrix ones diagonal describes contemporaneous interaction endogenous variables phi: \(k\)-dimensional vector log volatility innovation variances AR1 stochastic volatility: : \(k \times k\) lower triangular matrix ones diagonal describes contemporaneous interaction endogenous variables gamma_0: \(k\)-dimensional vector log volatility intercepts gamma_1: \(k\)-dimensional vector log volatility slopes Phi: \(k \times k\) log volatility innovation covariance matrix
